cross_domain_papers.title: Castellano et al. review connects voter model to spatial ecology
Berut et al. (2012) — Landauer limit verified experimentally at k_B T ln 2 ~ 3 zJ per bit
Beggs & Plenz (2003) — original neural avalanche paper establishing critical exponents
Maynard Smith & Price (1973) — ESS and cyclical dynamics in zero-sum games

Turing (1952) — original prediction λ ∝ √(D/k)
Newman (2002) proves the mathematical equivalence — FSS must apply if the equivalence holds
Bak, Tang & Wiesenfeld (1987) — BTW sandpile predicts τ = 3/2 (mean field)
WBE derives 3/4 from area-preserving branching — the recursion relation is structurally an RG equation at its fixed point
Landauer (1961) — irreversibility and heat generation in computation
Price (1970) establishes the general decomposition applicable to cultural traits
May (1972) provides stability criterion for LV-type competitive systems
Saw et al. 2017 — topological defects in epithelia determine cell fate; establishes the defect-function link
Bennett (1973) — theoretical demonstration that reversible computation has zero heat cost
Barabási & Albert (1999) - scale-free networks fragile to targeted hub attack; gamma ≤ 3 implies rapid connectivity collapse
Taylor et al. (1999) Nature — fractal dimension D ≈ 1.3-1.5 correlates with aesthetic preference for Pollock paintings
Schmidhuber (2010) — formal compression-progress theory of beauty and curiosity
Melott & Thomas (2009) - Ordovician extinction selectivity (depth-stratified survival) matches UV kill hypothesis; climate cooling is insufficient to explain the selectivity pattern
Melott et al. (2004) - quantitative GRB ozone depletion model; 10-second GRB at 2 kpc removes ~47% of ozone column; UV flux increase is lethal to phytoplankton
Hays et al. (1976) — 100 kyr power confirmed in delta-O18 record; eccentricity correlation established
Laskar et al. (2011) — chaotic orbital evolution; eccentricity forcing computed precisely
Feulner (2012) — stellar evolution and faint young Sun; background on solar forcing timescales

Peccei & Quinn (1977) - original PQ symmetry solving strong CP problem; axion as Nambu-Goldstone boson of broken U(1)_PQ
Lynden-Bell & Wood (1968) establish gravothermal catastrophe theory
Chandrasekhar (1935) polytrope stability — n=3 critical boundary
Fromang & Papaloizou (2007) — numerical evidence for α ~ Pm^{0.5} in shearing box
Hawking (1975) — black hole entropy = area/4, precursor to holographic principle

Sutton et al. (2000) policy gradient theorem: the update rule Δπ ∝ π · ∇V is exactly the replicator dynamics in the policy space. Therefore multi-agent policy gradient converges to the same fixed points as replicator dynamics — which includes Nash equilibria but preferentially reaches ESS under certain initialisation and learning rate conditions.
Jarzynski equality — theoretical basis for near-equilibrium efficiency measurement
Bustamante et al. — review of motor protein thermodynamics showing near-stall efficiency
Toyabe et al. — Maxwell's demon experiment validates stochastic thermodynamics

Feinberg (1989) shows that deficiency one networks with specific linkage class structure can admit exactly one positive steady state or exhibit multiple steady states depending on rate constants — establishing the topological design space for oscillator circuits.
Lenton et al. (2008) — tipping elements framework; early-warning theory
Lenton et al. (2018) tipping cascade probabilities for IAM calibration
Dakos et al. (2012) — AR1 and variance rise before critical transitions across ecology and Earth systems
Ryan et al. (2019) — climate-driven dengue expansion; incidence time series available for analysis
Nordhaus (2007) DICE baseline — deterministic damage function
Weitzman (2009) shows fat tails dramatically increase optimal carbon price
Weitzman dismal theorem — fat tails make expected damages unbounded
Howard & Sterner — damage function meta-analysis shows standard IAMs underestimate
Armstrong McKay et al. (2022) — exceeding 1.5°C global warming could trigger multiple tipping points
Olshausen & Field (1996) Nature — sparse coding of natural images yields Gabor-like basis functions
Bell & Sejnowski (1997) — ICA of natural images yields V1-like filters
Atick & Redlich (1992) — retina as Wiener filter
Mézard et al. (2002) — cavity method shows solution space shattering at α_c (RSB transition)

Lindsay (2020) review: transformer attention and divisive normalisation share the key property of normalised, input-dependent gating — but quantitative comparisons to single-unit neural data are lacking.
Helfrich elastic energy provides the theoretical framework for lipid-mediated interactions
Singer-Nicolson model describes proteins embedded in 2D fluid membrane
C. elegans PAR polarisation kinetics characterised quantitatively — demonstrates the transition is sharp, fast, and driven by a single wave

Farmer & Foley (2009) propose LV-type agent dynamics for markets

Pickett & Wilkinson (2015) — causal review of income inequality and health
Marmot (2005) — status syndrome: social gradient in health operates across full income distribution

Rutherford & Lindquist (1998) — Hsp90 buffers cryptic variation, demonstrating canalization-evolvability linkage

Stamenović & Ingber (2002) — tensegrity model quantitatively predicts cell mechanical response
Brockett (1972) — Lie algebra controllability condition (basis for bracket depth complexity)

OGY control theory: systems near bifurcation boundaries show critical slowing down (autocorrelation increases, variance increases). These statistical signatures of proximity to bifurcation have been observed in power systems before cascading failures.
Pastor-Satorras & Vespignani (2001) — scale-free networks have p_c → 0, making targeted vaccination critical
Bak & Tang (1989) — proposed earthquake-SOC connection
Sornette & Sammis (1995) — critical point model for fault rupture

Busse et al. (2010) — ODE model of IL-2/Treg dynamics predicts oscillatory behavior near stability margin
Domingo & Holland (1997) — formal Shannon equivalence; fidelity increase should allow genome expansion up to new capacity limit
Denison et al. (2011) — nsp14 knockout increases SARS-CoV mutation rate ~15x, confirming proofreading function
Pagel et al. (2007) Nature — basic vocabulary changes at rate consistent with neutral drift, not selection
Michel et al. (2011) Science — verb regularization follows neutral-like power law
Piantadosi et al. (2011) — word length follows surprisal across 10 languages, confirming optimal coding
Garrard et al. (2005) — lexical and semantic changes in pre-symptomatic Alzheimer's (Iris Murdoch novels)
Corcoran et al. (2018) — semantic coherence predicts psychosis conversion; methodology directly applicable to AD

Couzin et al. (2002) — schooling model shows collective evasion speed increases with reduced neighbor number k

Liao et al. (2003) — hydrodynamic coupling in fish schooling measured directly
Weiner & Addadi (1997) establish protein template role in polymorph control
Gal et al. (2013) show ACC precursor pathway in sea urchin polymorph selection

Rybenkov et al. (1993) established that topoisomerase II actively maintains below-equilibrium knot frequencies. If topoisomerase II activity declines with age (as documented for yeast), knot frequency should rise toward the equilibrium value — increasing topological stress.
Maroudas-Sacks et al. 2020 — defect charge specifies body-axis location in hydra
Müller et al. (2012) measured D_Nodal=2 and D_Lefty=40 um^2/s by FCS
Eilenberg & Mac Lane — category theory foundations enable these formalizations
Taylor & Jonker (1978) — replicator equation and cyclic attractors in zero-sum games
Piantadosi (2014) reviews evidence for coding efficiency account
Ferrer i Cancho & Solé (2003) show α=1 emerges at mutual-information maximum

Lum et al. (2013) Sci Rep — Mapper applied to Type 2 diabetes and other disease datasets; demonstrates generality of topology-based subtype discovery

Lenton et al. (2012) — review of tipping EWI caveats; null model requirements that any multivariate method must pass

Todorov & Jordan (2002) OFC framework predicts Kalman-like weighting
Churchland et al. (2012) rotational dynamics consistent with forward model
Levy (2008) Cognition — surprisal predicts reading times; establishes the basic relationship this hypothesis extends to ERP amplitude
Clark (2013) Behav Brain Sci — predictive coding framework motivating the precision-weighting modulation of prediction-error signals

Gardner et al. (2022): grid cells occupy a torus (β₁ = 2, β₂ = 1) — a non-trivial topology demonstrating that persistent homology reveals structure invisible to linear methods. Working memory representations are likely similarly structured but unexplored.
Raj et al. (2020) — network diffusion model seeded on entorhinal cortex predicts tau PET distribution
Buckner et al. (2009) — default mode network hubs overlap with earliest amyloid-PET positive regions
Vogel et al. (2019) — data-driven tau subtypes follow connectome architecture
Sporns et al. (2007) — hub identification methods in human brain networks
Chialvo (2010) — critical brain hypothesis review
Shew & Plenz (2013) — showed dynamic range and information capacity peak at criticality
Muñoz (2018) — comprehensive review of criticality in neural systems
Kass & Raftery (1995) — Bayes factor calibration; B > 10 = strong evidence against H_0
Doob (1949) asymptotic consistency — prior convergence guarantee
Kass & Raftery (1995) Bayes factors — finite-sample prior sensitivity analysis
Power et al. (2022) document sudden generalisation with a sharp front in test accuracy — consistent with a sharp continuous transition
Choromanska et al. (2015) connect DNN loss landscape to SK spin-glass free energy

Engler et al. (2006) show cells migrate toward stiffer substrates and that stiffness directs stem cell fate — establishing durotaxis as a real phenomenon with large effect sizes. Primary target organs of metastasis (liver ~1-2 kPa, lung ~2-5 kPa, bone ~25-40 kPa) are among the stiffest soft tissues in the body.
Bi et al. (2016) - vertex model predicts mean-field-like jamming transition at p0=3.81
Scheffer et al. (2009) — universal AR1 scaling near fold: AR1 ~ 1 - c*(mu_c - mu)^(1/2); this exponent 1/2 has not been measured from AMOC data

Boers (2021) — detected rising AR1 and variance in AMOC fingerprint (sea surface temperature gradient) 1870-2020; concluded AMOC approaching tipping; but did not fit fold-bifurcation scaling exponents
Google Quantum AI (2023) - surface code experiments approaching threshold; consistent with theoretical threshold value
Hopfield (1982) — associative memory energy model
Amit et al. (1985) — exact critical capacity alpha_c = 0.138 from replica theory

Loladze & Elser (2011) - ribosomal RNA stoichiometry constraint explains P-richness

With & Crist (1995) showed animal movement collapses near percolation threshold in fragmented landscapes
Harte et al. (2008) - METE equilibrium predictions work in stable forests; departures noted in disturbed sites
Nattel (2002) — AF rotor dynamics; gap junction remodelling reduces coupling K; Kuramoto desynchronization is the mechanism
Dorfler & Bullo (2012) — exact formula K_c = lambda_2(L)^(-1) where lambda_2 is the spectral gap of the network Laplacian; derived for power grids; directly applicable to cardiac coupling
Challet & Zhang (1997) - MG phase transition at alpha_c analogous to error threshold
Manrubia & Zanette (2002) PRE - explicit mapping of MG to quasispecies shows shared critical structure

Ghashghaie et al. (1996) — FX market cascade statistics match turbulence
Penrose Weyl curvature hypothesis - alternative cosmological account of the low-entropy origin

Gatenby et al. — adaptive therapy clinical foundation (passive model)

Engel et al. (2007) observe coherence signatures consistent with quantum transport
Rebentrost et al. (2009) ENAQT theoretical prediction with model bath

Isolating the internal dynamics from external forcing is the central methodological challenge.

The breeder's equation R = h^2 * S already implicitly contains the Fisher speed limit: V_A = h^2 * V_P (additive genetic variance). The response R per generation equals V_A * beta (selection gradient). This saturates the Fisher bound in infinite populations. The question is whether finite Ne and epistasis reduce R below V_A * beta in practice.

Long-term selection experiments (Illinois long-term selection for oil in maize, >100 generations; Drosophila body size experiments; E. coli LTEE) show sustained response over many generations, suggesting V_A is maintained and the Fisher bound is not permanently exceeded. Systematic comparison to the exact Fisher bound has not been done.

Epistasis and gene-by-environment interactions mean V_A (and hence the Fisher information) changes as allele frequencies change under selection. In strong selection, V_A may decline faster than the breeder's equation predicts — the "Bulmer effect." The Fisher speed limit is therefore not static; it co-evolves with the population. A static speed limit comparison may be systematically wrong, and the hypothesis requires fitting the dynamic Fisher information trajectory rather than a snapshot.

The history of physics shows successive simplification: Ptolemy's epicycles (hundreds of parameters) → Kepler's laws (3 laws, ~6 parameters) → Newton's gravity (1 law, 2 parameters, predicts all planetary motion). This is compression in action: each successor theory has demonstrably shorter description length for the same observational data.

In modern physics, the Standard Model Lagrangian encodes all known particle physics in approximately one printed page. Competing theories (SUSY, technicolour) require longer Lagrangians without confirmed additional predictions — consistent with MDL preferring the Standard Model until new data compels the longer description.

Predictive accuracy (likelihood) may drive theory selection independently of theory complexity. The Copernican vs. Ptolemaic system had roughly equal predictive accuracy with contemporary instrumentation; the Copernican selection may have been driven more by simplicity (shorter description) than accuracy — but this historical argument is not a controlled test.

Contact networks in institutions may be sufficiently heterogeneous (staff hub nodes) that the random-graph FSS formula (nu=1) does not apply — the system may be in a different universality class or crossover regime where FSS corrections are much smaller or larger than predicted. Only empirical validation can distinguish these cases.

Registered Reports (peer review before data collection, publication guaranteed) show ~85% replication rate in meta-analyses vs. ~40% for standard publications in psychology (Scheel et al., 2021). This provides the strongest individual-study evidence that preregistration increases confirmatory validity.

OSF preregistration rates by sub-discipline in psychology correlate weakly with estimated replication rates in existing multi-lab projects (Many Labs 1–5), but no formal regression analysis has been published.

Fields with high preregistration rates (clinical trials via ClinicalTrials.gov) also have high non-compliance rates; preregistration does not prevent selective outcome reporting if compliance is not enforced.

Floer (1988) constructed an infinite-dimensional Morse theory on the loop space of a symplectic manifold; its homology groups HF*(M, ω) count pseudoholomorphic cylinders. In parallel, the Gutzwiller trace formula connects classical periodic orbits to quantum energy levels with quantum corrections. The Arnol'd conjecture (proved by Floer) gives a lower bound on fixed points of Hamiltonian maps equal to the sum of Betti numbers — suggesting Floer homology computes quantum degeneracy structure.

For integrable systems, the Bohr-Sommerfeld quantization condition (∮ p dq = nℏ) is exactly the condition that the Lagrangian tori are Floer-geometric objects satisfying a topological constraint. This suggests Floer homology extends Bohr-Sommerfeld to non-integrable systems.

The connection between Floer homology and physical quantum spectra requires going beyond the semiclassical approximation; mathematical rigour requires the manifold to be compact (no scattering states) — limiting direct application to physical Hamiltonians with continuous spectra.

Nidovirus genome sizes range from 13 kb (arterivirus) to 41 kb (planarian secretory cell nidovirus) — the largest known RNA genomes. All large nidoviruses (>20 kb) encode an nsp14-like proofreading exonuclease; all small nidoviruses (<20 kb) lack it. This discrete jump at ~20 kb is quantitatively consistent with the Shannon capacity prediction: for typical RNA virus mutation rates (10^-4/base), the maximum supported genome size without proofreading is ~10 kb; with 10-15x fidelity improvement, the capacity-limited genome size rises to 20-30 kb.

The nsp14 exonuclease may primarily function as a cap methyltransferase (its N-terminal domain) rather than proofreading — the proofreading function may be incidental. Additionally, the genome size increase in coronaviruses may be driven by selective pressure for specific gene functions (spike protein complexity, immune evasion) rather than by information-theoretic capacity expansion. The Shannon-optimality hypothesis requires that the fidelity and genome size co-evolved, which must be tested by reconstructing ancestral nidovirus sequences.
